I love and respect you guys, and I will remain your quant. I’ll do my best to help as long as I’m here.
You always need to be critical. Whatever I write should be discussed, reviewed, expanded, or corrected if I’ve made a mistake. That will happen sometimes, there’s always room for that.

comment.body:
To that end, read back on a prior weeks post seemed fitting to share again: "Strategy
As I mentioned before, in this slow spot-decaying environment, I still see an intraday call-side edge and a short-term short-vol strategy edge, while holding March puts to hedge against decay. We must be extremely cautious in this type of market, avoid chasing, and wait for the right moment to jump in and out with our money—as if we were never there. 
We'll continue going day by day until OpEx, watching closely for sudden convex increases in the pricing of tail SPX puts and tail VIX calls.
I’m bullish into EOY."

comment.body:
Example Conclusion:
Your approach is all about understanding both the broader speed profile (which sets the tone) and the local effects that can trigger short-term moves. The neutral-to-suppressive overall market environment means we shouldn't expect a major rally, but we could see local squeezes or price fluctuations based on volatility dynamics and dealer positioning.
Does this interpretation align with your view? And, are there any other factors you’re watching that might shift the outlook?

comment.body: Don't be too harsh on yourself. This stuff is pretty complex. But in order to get the best out of this subscription you need to develop an intuition and a knowledge base about these things. Study the greeks one by one. ChatGPT and the like can be very valuable as well (although some stuff needs to be double checked). I think a lot of the confusion also arises due to a different mindset. There is no need to trade everyday and there is no need to catch every turn. You will burn yourself and you capital up way too quick this way. A lot of the more experienced people know this (and frankly most of them, including myself) learned the hard way. People overestimate how much of success in trading has nothing to do with strategy but with emotional control, patience, discipline etc.

comment.body: I think the best way to start to get an understanding of the Greeks is to draw the price. Then the delta underneath, then using the tangent on the delta to draw the gamma you would expect, from the gamma curve you can then draw the speed. It sort of instantly explains where and when you would expect to positive speed etc. a similar approach can be used on other Greeks. It’s a good place to start, I personally am new to most of this so no expert but that was helpful for me. So I could begin to grasp some of the chat.

And feel there is a great community here that seems fed up with simple retailers like myself. I'll do better.
comment.raw_body: Let me be the first to apologize as a newer member here within the last week asking some far to simple questions based on my limited and new understanding of the greeks. Reading back through some initial posts it is far beyond my comprehension of math without question which makes understanding a lot of what is shared here seem incomprehensible. That's not on anyone else to shoulder the burden. So I hope I don't bring that energy to anyone here. I'm very much wanting to learn with the tools at my disposal. And feel there is a great community here that seems fed up with simple retailers like myself. I'll do better.

comment.body: yes. or even better: ask chat gpt to code you a black and scholes model in excel, including the graphs of the greeks. And then just start playing around with the parameters until you develop some intuition. That's the best way to learn it

comment.body: Also for the more intuitive aspects of options I can recommend moontower.ai. Kris is a former option market maker and has a lot of good (free) posts on the basic intuitions behind options and also some really valuable technical insights

comment.body: And this is probably the one thing everybody who is serious about being here needs to do. Once you understand the greeks for a single option you can start applying it to option portfolios (like Alma does)

comment.body:
Last week someone kindly referenced this document as providing a good explanation of dealer hedging mechanics https://squeezemetrics.com/download/The_Implied_Order_Book.pdf
I agree it is quite helpful.  It's entirely consistent with and dovetails nicely into Alma's description delta, gamma, and vanna, and relates that to dealer hedging mechanics.  Perhaps some others will also find it helpful in putting the pieces together.  And referencing both Mike's and Chris's posts immediately above, this "Implied Order Book" document has good illustrations of the before/after probability distributions with changes in the parameter that drives each of the greeks, and if you really study it and think about it, and study it again, and think some more, the picture starts to come into focus.
comment.raw_body:

Perhaps some others will also find it helpful in putting the pieces together.  And referencing both Mike's and Chris's posts immediately above, this "Implied Order Book" document has good illustrations of the before/after probability distributions with changes in the parameter that drives each of the greeks, and if you really study it and think about it, and study it again, and think some more, the picture starts to come into focus.
